coredata.dc:title: Molecularly imprinted optical probe based on in-situ CoFe2O4/CNDs for monitoring 2,4,6-para trinitrophenol in aqueous medium with integrated smartphone detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Murugan, Komal
Natarajan, Abirami
Murthy Sridevi, Vetrinelaven
coredata.dc:description: Assessing TNP in actual water samples is essential to reduce its harmful impacts on human health and aquatic ecosystems. This study details the development of an innovative and sustainable dual-mode optical biosensor that employs Cobalt ferrite magnetic nanoparticles (CF-MNPs), Carbon nanodots (CNDs), and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) for the precise and sensitive identification of 2,4,6-para trinitrophenol (TNP) in water-based environments. The plant-derived CNDs from Piper longum demonstrate improved fluorescence properties, leading to a notable enhancement in the sensor's performance. The CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIP composite improves the selectivity and sensitivity of the optical sensing probe by promoting efficient electron transfer with TNP. The dual-mode biosensing platform integrates fluorescence and colorimetric detection, leveraging the peroxidase-mimicking activity of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs to oxidize TMB for a visual colorimetric response. The biosensor demonstrates exceptional analytical capabilities, reaching a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.09 nM (fluorescence) and 3.07 nM (colorimetry). Furthermore, the analysis of real samples exhibited remarkable reliability, achieving a recovery rate of 101 %. To improve portability and field applicability, an RGB-based smartphone sensing method was incorporated, achieving a limit of detection 10 nM, facilitating rapid and economical on-site identification of TNP. This study presents a sustainable, highly selective, and ultrasensitive dual-mode optical biosensor, providing a strong foundation for real-time environmental monitoring of water contaminants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Peroxidase
Portable
Rapid
Smartphone
Sustainable

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorometric binding constant calculation and LOD:
Application of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs as a smartphone-based optical bioassay for TNP
Colorimetric binding constant calculation for TNP

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was regarding the published Indian Patent (2022) \u201CNANO COMPOSITE AND A PROCESS FOR ITS PREPARATION\u201D (Application No. 202241051985 A). We acknowledge the Centre for Sustainable Material and Surface Metamorphosis, Chennai Institute of Technology, India, for partial funding (Funding number: CIT/CSMSM/2025/RP004) and acknowledge the Nanotechnology Research Centre (NRC), SRMIST, for providing the research facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM image, (b) lattice fringe (Inset: TEM image at 1000 pm dimension), and (c) SAED pattern of CNDs. (d, e) HR-TEM image and (f) SAED pattern and (g) EDAX spectrum of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs.
Pictorial images of SEM analysis (a) CF-MNPs, (b) CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs, and (c) CF-MNPs/CNDs/NIPs.
XRD spectra of (a) CF-MNPs and (b) CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs. FT-IR spectra (c) CNDs, (d) CF-MNPs and (e) CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs.
XPS analysis of CF-MNPs (a) Survey, (b) Co 2p, (c) Fe 2p, and (d) O1s.

(a) Absorbance spectra of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs. (b) An Independent excitation wavelength of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs. (c) FL response spectrum. (d) Elution of TNP from CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs. (e) FL intensity vs CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs (mg) with the fluorescence images.
Optimization of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs for TNP detection under the effect of (a) solvents, (b) NaCl concentrations, (c) pH, and (d) response time.

(a) Fluorescence spectral response of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs vs Conc. of TNP and (b) & (c) Linearity fit of F
0
/F vs Concentration of TNP (0.5 to 500 nM and 0.5–150 nM, respectively). (d) Fluorescence lifetime spectra. (e) Fluorescence spectral response of CF-MNPs/CNDs/NIPs vs Conc. of TNP and (f) & (g) Linearity fit of F
0
/F vs Concentration of TNP 0.5 to 500 nM and 0.5–260 nM, respectively.

Fluorescence image of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs with different concentrations of TNP under daylight (a) and UV light (b). (c, d) Selectivity analysis and quenching efficiency of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs with TNP and other interfering analytes. (e) Reproducibility studies (number of cycles vs CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs.
The proposed CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs fluorescent assay-based smartphone detection platform for sensing TNP.

(a) Mechanism of peroxidase activity of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs, (b) UV–Vis spectrum representing the peroxidase behavior of the synthesized samples. (c) EPR spectrum of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs nanozyme (d) Reactive oxygen species analysis. (e) Absorbance spectra of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs vs Conc. of TNP and (f) & (g) Linearity fit of absorbance vs Concentration of TNP 0.5 to 200 nM and 0.5–24 nM, respectively. (h) Visual image of colorimetric sensing of TNP.
Synthesis of CF/MNPs/CNDs/MIPs with TNP and without TNP.
Zeta potential values of the synthesized samples.

head.ce:title: Molecularly imprinted optical probe based on in-situ CoFe2O4/CNDs for monitoring 2,4,6-para trinitrophenol in aqueous medium with integrated smartphone detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Sustainable Materials and Surface Metamorphosis, Chennai Institute of Technology, Chennai, Tamil Nadu 600 069, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Sustainable Materials and Surface Metamorphosis

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Sustainable Materials and Surface Metamorphosis, Chennai Institute of Technology, Chennai, Tamil Nadu-600 069, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603 203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu-603 203, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Assessing TNP in actual water samples is essential to reduce its harmful impacts on human health and aquatic ecosystems. This study details the development of an innovative and sustainable dual-mode optical biosensor that employs Cobalt ferrite magnetic nanoparticles (CF-MNPs), Carbon nanodots (CNDs), and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) for the precise and sensitive identification of 2,4,6-para trinitrophenol (TNP) in water-based environments. The plant-derived CNDs from Piper longum demonstrate improved fluorescence properties, leading to a notable enhancement in the sensor's performance. The CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIP composite improves the selectivity and sensitivity of the optical sensing probe by promoting efficient electron transfer with TNP. The dual-mode biosensing platform integrates fluorescence and colorimetric detection, leveraging the peroxidase-mimicking activity of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs to oxidize TMB for a visual colorimetric response. The biosensor demonstrates exceptional analytical capabilities, reaching a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.09 nM (fluorescence) and 3.07 nM (colorimetry). Furthermore, the analysis of real samples exhibited remarkable reliability, achieving a recovery rate of 101 %. To improve portability and field applicability, an RGB-based smartphone sensing method was incorporated, achieving a limit of detection 10 nM, facilitating rapid and economical on-site identification of TNP. This study presents a sustainable, highly selective, and ultrasensitive dual-mode optical biosensor, providing a strong foundation for real-time environmental monitoring of water contaminants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: A significant number of researchers have turned their attention to the global impact of environmental pollutants due to the illegal use of explosives. To mitigate the health risks faced by living organisms, it is vital to separate and eliminate these pollutants [1,2]. TNP (2,4,6-trinitrophenol) is the most significant explosive (nitro compounds) used in pharmaceutical and dye-based industries, aside from applications such as rocket fuel production. [3,4] It causes adverse effects like cancer, skin irritation, breathing failure, etc. Despite its environmental persistence and significant health and safety concerns, TNP is not widely included in existing environmental regulations or routinely monitored as a priority pollutant. The permissible concentration of TNP in groundwater is 0.001 mg/L, as stated by the World Health Organization [WHO]. [4] This regulatory gap highlights the necessity for efficient detection strategies. Current methodologies, including HPLC and GC–MS, although precise, typically necessitate advanced instrumentation and comprehensive sample preparation, rendering them unsuitable for on-site or real-time analysis. The identified limitations underscore the necessity for the development of portable, sensitive, and selective detection tools that can identify TNP at trace levels within complex environmental matrices, such as lentic water.

The usual ways to find nitro compounds, especially 2,4,6-para trinitrophenol, are mass spectrometry, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays, chromatography, electrochemistry, voltammetry, solid-phase extraction, and SERS. [5–7] The unique physical and chemical properties of the analyte, such as high polarity, high boiling point, and high volatility, cause the detection methods mentioned earlier to work poorly, showing low sensitivity and selectivity when identifying nitro compounds. In addition to these specific methodologies, the electrochemical process entails intricate procedures and costly equipment. These factors have led to the need for a highly accurate, cost-effective, and convenient analytical technique for nitro compound detection. The optical (fluoro/colorimetric) detection technique is a speedy, cost-effective, highly sensitive, and selective method. [8,9] This method is appealing because it is easy to use, can be expanded for larger needs, and can handle a lot of wastewater without creating harmful by-products, unlike the adsorption process. [10,11] The optical detection technique incorporates one or more constituents with varying properties that work together synergistically for the sensing application.

In recent times, nanomaterials have functioned as a mainstream in the field of sensor research, and among the diverse types, Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are versatile and sustainable nanomaterials. CNDs, 0-D nanostructured material with a spheroidal morphology embedded with sp2 and sp3 carbon atoms, lie within a diameter ranging from 1 to 10 nm. [12] They exhibit several characteristics, such as photostability, catalytic activity, high resistance, size confinement, high quantum yield, and fluorescence emission properties. [13] Furthermore, the extensive surface area and surface states promote interactions with the surrounding environment, allowing for effective detection via mechanisms like fluorescence quenching, which is a beneficial trait in chemical sensors. CNDs offer a significant advantage due to their facile and efficient synthesis, which can be scaled up using a diverse range of carbon-rich precursors. Utilizing naturally abundant, accessible, cost-effective, and renewable materials can replace conventional chemicals in the CND synthesis process, thereby promoting environmental sustainability. Although previous studies have reported plant-derived CNDs from sources such as tea leaves, orange peels, and Curcuma zedoaria, there appears to be no literature on using Piper longum as a precursor. [14] This nanomaterial plays a vital role in applications such as catalysis, optical/electrochemical sensors, photovoltaics, energy storage, and conversion. [15]

Lately, there has been a significant rise in the study of ferrite nanoparticles for use in analytical applications, especially when paired with quantum dots, carbon dots, metal-organic frameworks, and covalent-organic frameworks as sensing tools. [16] CF-MNPs (cobalt ferrite magnetic nanoparticles), a type of spinel ferrite, represent a highly appealing class of materials capable of imparting novel properties to their composites. The CF-MNPs exhibit adjustable optical and magnetic properties influenced by their chemical composition and tailored for specific applications, such as sensors or photocatalysts. [17,18] CF-MNPs exhibit an inverse spinel structure characterized by divalent metallic atoms residing in the octahedral sites, while a trivalent atom is positioned at the tetrahedral sites, classifying it as a p-type magnetic material. [19,20] Cobalt ferrite exhibits remarkable potential applications across diverse domains, including photocatalysis, sensors, electronics, biomedicine, and magnetic recordings. [21] In the field of sensing, the CF-MNPs/CNDs function as nanotags to boost the sensing performance of the optical probe sensitivity and as a substrate to the host receptor where the required selectivity can be achieved.

MIPs (Molecularly Imprinted Polymers) are a diverse receptor in the optical sensing approach because of their specific recognition, i.e., the “LOCK AND KEY” mechanism. [22,23] MIPs are cross-linked polymers with specific pores formed by using a target molecule with respect to their size and shape, followed by the cross-linking agent via polymerization reaction. The polymer matrix of the MIPs forms tailor-made binding sites with the memory of the target molecule. The functional group at these recognition sites in the MIP structure attaches to the specific target molecule through hydrogen bonding, covalent bonding, and non-covalent or electrostatic interactions. [24,25] MIPs' unique features, like reusability, thermal stability, rigidity, and flexibility, make them mainstream sensor receptors. In summary, MIPs can gradually improve stability and selectivity by acting as a receptor, while CF-MNPs/CNDs serve as a substrate for the host receptor, thereby increasing sensitivity for accurate TNP detection.

Our study employs a Stern-Volmer plot to investigate the optical sensing phenomenon of both fluorescence and colorimetric detection of TNP using the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs probe. This study introduces a novel biosourced approach—utilizing Piper longum for the synthesis of CNDs and their unique integration into an MIP platform—thereby enhancing fluorescence and specificity for TNP detection, a combination that has not been previously explored. It will provide insights into the fluorescence quenching mechanism and peroxidase-like catalytic activity under varying conditions for the identification of TNPs. Further, the detection limits were identified via the RGB values measured by the color detector software via smartphone. The CNDs derived from Piper longum have not been reported yet. The combination of the novel plant-derived CNDs with CF-MNPs and the developed CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIP displays a more sensitive and selective response toward TNPs than the previously reported fluorescent probes.

Cobalt chloride tetrahydrate (CoCl2.4H2O) (99 %), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6H2O) (97 %), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) (97 %), aqueous ammonia (Aq. NH3) (30 %), ethanol (C2H5OH) (99 %), acetone (CH3COCH3) (99 %) was purchased from Avra Chemicals Pvt. Ltd., India. 3-aminopropyl ethoxy silane (C9H23NO3Si) (98 %), tetra ethoxy silane (C8H20O4Si) (98 %), acetonitrile (CH3CN) (99 %), acetic acid (C2H4O2) (99.9 %), Hydroquinone (C6H6O2) (99 %), catechol (C6H6O2) (99 %), 2-nitrophenol (C6H5NO3) (99 %), 3-nitroaniline (C6H6N2O2) (99 %), Whatman filter paper, Teflon-lined autoclave, centrifuge tubes, and glass apparatus were brought from SISCON, Pvt. Ltd., India. The leaf Piper longum was collected from the nearby market.

Hi-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) JEM-2100 Plus, Imaging with EDAX, SAED pattern (200 kV – accelerating voltage), and Hi-Resolution Scanning Electron Microscope (HRSEM) Thermoscientific Apreo S (15,000 V) were utilized to characterize the surface morphology. Fourier Transform InfraRed (FT-IR) Spectroscopy was performed on a SHIMADZU IRTRACER 100 with a wavenumber range (7800–350 cm−1). X-ray diffraction Spectrometer (XRD) PANalytical 2θ = 10 to 80̊ (Cu Kα radiation wavelength = 1.54 Å) provides detailed information regarding the crystallinity nature of the material. X-ray photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) ULVAC PHI (VersaProbe III) Al Ka radiation as the excitation source identifies the elemental composition within a material. UV–Vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU UV 5600 PLUS) within the range of 200–800 nm investigates the absorption of ultraviolet (UV) and visible light by molecules. A photoluminescence spectrometer (Flurolog HORIBA) will study excitation (λ = 318 nm), emission (λ = 420 nm), steady-state fluorescence, and quantum yield. The particle charge has been measured using a Zeta sizer. Color detector software is installed on the smartphone.

Piper longum belongs to the Piperaceae family, commonly called pippli. Their leaves were cleaned with running tap water and dried in the shade. Mechanical grinding was used to powder the leaves. In 100 ml of ethanol, 25 g coarse leaf powder was mixed at room temperature for 2 h and then filtered to obtain the leaf extract.

In the hydrothermal process, 45 ml of Piper longum leaf extract and 2 ml of a 1 M solution of aqueous NH3 were mixed, then moved to a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 180 °C for ten hours. Following the reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool to ambient temperature before being cautiously opened. The color change from light green to dark brown visually proved the development of nitrogen-doped carbon dots. The resulting solution was filtered using Whatman filter paper and centrifuged for 40 min at 10,000 rpm to produce pure nitrogen-doped carbon dots free of unreacted organic moieties and big carbon residues (Scheme 1 ).

The final hybrid nanocomposite CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs were synthesized via sol-gel polymerization. Initially, TNP (1 mmol) was dissolved in 75 ml of ethanol, to which 300 μl of ammonia were added, and dispersed. With 15 min of intervals, 22 ml of the CF-MNPs colloidal solution was added and the solution was stirred for an additional 10 min. Then, 2.5 ml of CNDs, and ammonia solution (1 ml) followed by APTES (2 mmol) were added and stirred for 30 min. In the final stage, TEOS (6 mmol) was added, and the mixture was continued to be stirred at room temperature for 20 h. The resulting precipitate is further washed with solvents (acetic acid: ethanol) to remove the TNP and to obtain our final product CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs. CF-MNPs/CNDs/NIPs were synthesized under the same conditions as CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs but without the inclusion of TNP. Scheme 3 represents the synthesis of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs before and after the elution of TNP.

HRTEM analysis was used to predict the surface morphology of the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs. Fig. 1 (a) shows the uniform spherical shape of CNDs with a well-dispersed nature with an average particle size of 4.75 nm. Fig. 1(b) shows the interplanar spacing of about 0.30 nm and Fig.1 (c) shows the SAED pattern in which the formation of the clear circle confirms the amorphous nature of the CNDs. Fig. 1(d) is TEM images of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs and Fig. 1(e) is the zoomed-in version of Fig. 1(d). It represented that the CNDs and CF-MNPs are well engraved on the surface of the polymer matrix. The SAED pattern in Fig. 1(f) shows slightly enhanced crystallinity compared with the CNDs. The EDAX spectrum in Fig. 1(g) depicts that the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs have the existence of C, N, O, Co, Fe, and Si.

The SEM images of CF-MNPs, CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs, and CF-MNPs/CNDs/NIPs are presented in Fig. 2 (a-c). The CF-MNPs show a slightly rough surface and MIP and NIP nanocomposite show an unevenly agglomerated sponge-like surface. These results portray that the surface morphology has been influenced by the template during the polymerization process.

XRD data was performed to study the crystalline/amorphous nature of synthesized CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs. The peaks of CF-MNPs appeared at 31.04°, 36.5°, 38.50°, 44.47°, 48.46°, and 64.9° with corresponding lattice planes (220), (311), (222), (400), (331), and (531) in Fig. 3(a). JCPDS card No. 22–1086 exactly matches the peak coordinates [26]. Additional peaks of Fe2O3 at 33.4°, 50.08°, and 68.9° belong to reflections (241), (024), and (208) [JCPDS card No. 33–0664]. [27,28]. The favorable heating conditions that were present during the synthesis of cobalt ferrite were responsible for the formation of the secondary phase of Fe2O3. By using the Debye-Scherrer formula, D = Kλ / βcosθ the size of the CF-MNPs was calculated to be 7.2 nm. The formation of the nanocomposite CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs by the XRD spectra is presented in Fig. 3(b). The synthesized CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs possess peaks at (300), (220), (311), (222), (400), and (531) with 2θ values of 28.6°, 31.4°, 36.2°,38.30°, 44.6° and 64.5° respectively, for CF-MNPs. The sharp peak at 20.1° (2θ) related to the 002 plane, as well as other minor peaks at 10.4° and 22.1°, is attributed to the CNDs regarding our previously published work [4,29]. Moreover, the broad diffraction peak indicates that the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs are less amorphous.

FT-IR spectrum of CNDs is presented in Fig. 3(c). Functional groups such as -OH and -NH correspond to the broad adsorption band that appeared at 3000–3500 cm−1. This represents the interconnected hydrogen bonds in the CNDs. The band at 1629 cm−1, 1401 cm−1, 1222 cm−1 and 1130 cm−1 corresponds to -C=C, -C-O-C, -C=O and -C-N stretching vibrations correspondingly. The 1580 cm−1 absorption peak is assigned to -NH groups [30]. CF-MNPs FTIR spectrum in Fig. 3(d) the appearance of the band at 3439 cm−1 corresponds to the presence of a hydroxyl group on the cobalt ferrite surface. The bands at 1651 cm−1 and 1431 cm−1 show the presence of CO asy and sym vibrations [31]. The peaks at 422 cm−1 and 549 cm−1 confirm the FeCo stretching modes [32]. The peaks at 1054 cm−1 and 762 cm−1 are ascribed to Si-O-Si stretching vibration and SiO symmetric stretching vibrations. The broad peaks at 3169 cm−1 and 2913 cm−1 correspond to OH and CH stretching vibrations. The characteristic peaks of CNDs and CF-MNPs are reflected in the FT-IR spectrum of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs, as shown in Fig. 3(e). The characteristic peak of TNP at 1495 cm-1, which belongs to the -NO2 moieties, is absent at CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs due to the elution process. This confirms that the TNP has been completely eluted from CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs.

The oxidation state of the CF-MNPs was recorded using wide energy range XPS spectra. Fig. 4(a) shows the full survey spectrum. The peak at 781.6 eV, 715.2 eV, and 530.8 eV is attributed to Co2p, Fe2p3/2, and O1s [33]. The C1s peak in the survey spectrum with a peak value of 283.84 eV corresponds to carbon contaminants present on the surface of the tested samples. The deconvoluted spectra of Co2p in Fig. 4(b), peak at approximately 796.24 eV [Co2p1/2] and 780.58 eV [Co2p3/2], indicates that the valence state of cobalt has the form of +2 instead of +3. The peaks at 803.8 and 788.68 eV refer to the satellite peak signals. The deconvoluted spectra of Fe2p are represented in Fig. 4(c). The binding energy at 725.63 eV is assigned to Fe2p1/2 and 712.89 eV for Fe2p3/2 with a satellite peak at 719.51 eV. The peaks in Fig. 4(d) show the deconvoluted spectra of the O1s spectrum with peak values at 531.9 eV and 533.1 eV ascribed to metal oxide and oxygen defects respectively. This confirms the successful formation of CF-MNPs to undergo polymerization to form the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs.

The optical properties of the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs and CF-MNPs/ CNDs/ NIPs were investigated by the absorbance and fluorescence spectrum. The absorption peak at 257 nm indicates the pi-pi* transition of the aromatic heterocycle, and the peak at 319 nm is ascribed to the n-pi* transition of the CO group as depicted in Fig. 5(a). The presence of nitrogen atoms imparts an electron-rich characteristic to the CNDs, facilitating energy transfer to the excited pi⁎ of sp2 orbitals and ultimately improving fluorescence. The fluorescence emission spectra were taken under different excitation wavelengths [the wavelength ranging from 295 to 320 nm]. The emission wavelength has not been shifted to a higher wavelength; from this, it is obvious from Fig. 5(b) that the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs have an excitation-independent optical property. Moreover, the quantum yield of the CNDs was found to be 20 % (quinine sulfate is used as a reference), showing the fluorescence emission peak at about 421 nm when excited at 318 nm, which was in accordance with the CNDs. The impact of the template (TNP) on the fluorescence intensity was analyzed, and the results are presented in Fig. 5(c). The fluorescence intensity of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs without the template shows a higher maximum intensity than the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs with the template. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs without the template was restored to 92.5 % of CF-MNPs/CNDs/NIPs (reference material). This indicates that the TNP is effectively washed out from the polymer matrix. Further, the elution of TNP from the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs is confirmed by the UV–vis absorbance spectra represented in Fig. 5(d).

The high concentration of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs can influence the spectral measurements (Fig. 5e). When an excitation beam is passed on a highly concentrated solution, the molecules on the surface will emit radiation. In this regard, the fluorescence and absorbance studies of the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs were recorded at different concentrations (10–100 mg). The CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs, when exposed to UV light, show a strong blue fluorescence (FL) emission, even up to 100 mg (shown in the inset figures). This portrays that the fluorescence intensity is not affected by the increase in the concentration of the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs.

The optical (fluorescence and absorbance) wavelength or intensity can be affected using various solvents. The FL intensity of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs was recorded with different solvents (DMF, methanol, ethanol, deionized water, and acetone) to select the appropriate solvent for the titration experiments. Fig. 6(a) depicts that the FL intensity of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs is high only when it is dispersed in deionized water. The ionic strength has some effect on the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs fluorescence intensity; hence, it was investigated with different concentrations of NaCl (0.5–2 M). The NaCl (ionic strength) has shown zero influence on the fluorescence intensity of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIP (Fig. 6(b)) and in the detection of TNP, so it can be potentially applied for analytical applications. The CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs detection capacity and sensitivity are greatly affected by the different pH of the system. The sensing potential of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs for the TNP is recorded at different pH levels [ranging from 2.0 to 10.0]. The quenching response obtained at pH 6.5 and pH 7 is maximum with a time of 9 mins for CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIP is revealed in Fig. 6(c and d). Since the quenching efficiency decreases when the pH is increased, this is due to the damage to the binding sites.

The sensing potential of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs toward the TNP was investigated via fluorescence and colorimetric titration experiments. Hereby, the fluorescence experiments were conducted with various concentrations of TNP in an aqueous solution, phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), against CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs (excitation wavelength-318 nm). The significant addition of TNP solution to the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs leads to quenching of the fluorescence intensity, as displayed in Fig. 7(a). The rapid decrease in the fluorescence intensity in the presence of TNP suggests efficient electron transfer from the electron-rich CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs to the high electron-deficient TNP. The efficient donor-acceptor interaction and the specific recognition sites on the MIP hybrid nanocomposites cause the drastic quenching in the fluorescence intensity in the existence of TNP. Moreover, this result demonstrates that CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs experienced a quicker and more efficient decrease in the fluorescence intensity with the TNP as that of CF-MNPs/CNDs/NIPs due to the lack of imprinted sites for the TNP. Further, CF-MNPs/ CNDs/ MIPs undergo zeta potential analysis, and the results are presented in Table 1 . The CF-MNPs zeta potential value is −23.2 mV. At the same time, for CF-MNPs/ CNDs/MIPs, it is – 3.02 mV, which indicates a negative potential. As per the previously reported literature, the bare CNDs possess a zeta potential value of −1.00 eV [8]. The zeta potential value of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs after the introduction of TNP showed a greater negative value of −15.04 mV. This is due to the significantly strong TNP adsorbing onto the composite surface, which introduces additional negative charges. Since TNP is deprotonated (picrate anion) at pH 7, it contributes a negative charge upon binding, making the zeta potential more negative. The CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs composite contains amine (-NH₂) and other cationic sites, TNP could be binding via electrostatic attraction before increasing surface negativity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric binding constant calculation and LOD:
ce:section.ce:para:
The binding constant calculation and the detection limit are the crucial parameters for the sensing application. Therefore, the binding constant and LOD calculation have been performed and determined for CF-MNPs, CNDs, CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs, and CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs. The binding constant calculation was carried out using the Stern-Volmer equation to prove the excellent sensitivity of the synthesized fluorescent probe:
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
C TNP

The fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of TNP is represented as F
0 and F, K
sv is the Stern-Volmer constant, and C
TNP is the TNP concentration. Fig. 7(b) illustrates the fluorescence response of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs over a linear concentration range of 0–500 nM. The linearity plot in Fig. 7(c) illustrates a relationship between the change in fluorescence intensity vs TNP concentration. From the plot, the determination coefficient (R2) was calculated to be 0.99588, with a well-fitted linear equation F
0
/F = 1.00601 + 0.00218[C TNP ]. The linear plot based on the Stern-Volmer equation between F
0
/F of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs against the increasing concentration of TNP demonstrates that quenching (dynamic and static) is responsible for diminishing fluorescence intensity. The possibility of the quenching mechanism could be determined through fluorescence lifetime (τ) decay profile studies represented in Fig.7(d) and the fluorescence decay lifetimes using an appropriate exponential decay fitting model in Fig. S1. The τ value of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs without TNP (τ is 6.52 ns) and CF-MNPs/CNDs/NIPs (τ is 6.09 ns) is lower than after the addition of TNP (τ is 6.50 ns), which confirms the static quenching process (refer to Table 2 ). The lifetime of the fluorescent probe remains unchanged (may be slightly changed) even after the addition of the targeted analyte (TNP); the process is termed static quenching [34]. In static quenching, a ground-state complex is formed due to intermolecular dimers between the donor (electron-rich fluorophore) and acceptor (electron-deficient quencher) with an altered absorption spectrum, leading to fluorescence suppression without significantly altering the excited-state lifetime.

Fig. 7(e) represents the fluorescence response of CF-MNPs/CNDs/NIPs over a linear concentration range of 0–500 nM. The linearity plot in Fig. 7(f) and 7(g) illustrates that the determination coefficient (R2) was found to be 0.94655, with a fitted equation F
0
/F = 1.0128 + 0.00022[C TNP ]. The K
sv values for CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs and CF-MNPs/CNDs/NIPs are 0.00218 and 0.00022, respectively. The K
sv value of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs is higher than the CF-MNPs/CNDs/NIPs, indicating the excellent binding capacity of MIPs composites with imprinted sites for the TNP molecule. The imprinting factor (IF) can be calculated based on the ratio of the K
sv values of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs and CF-MNPs/CNDs/NIPs, yielding an obtained IF of 10.9. This imprinting factor value suggests that the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs have good affinity and site fidelity toward the target molecule (TNP). This happens due to the effective and vital interaction of functional monomer, APTES ((3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane), with the TNP molecule. APTES functional monomers form specific complexes with TNP via non-covalent hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction, impacting the affinity between TNP and APTES. The sol-gel polymerization process entails the development of an intricate complex between the APTES and TNP, followed by crosslinking with a TEOS (Tetraethoxysilane) to lock the TNP in a specific recognition site. This results in the formation of a strongly interconnected and inflexible polymer. At this point, it was predicted that CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs would show high sensing efficiency. Based on this, the LOD was calculated using the formula:
(2)
LOD =
3 ∗
σ / S
σ is the standard deviation, and S is the slope (calibration curve). The detection limits of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs and CF-MNPs/CNDs/NIPs are 1.09 nM and 69 nM for the TNP for the fluorescence titration experiments. Table S1 compiles literature data regarding detection limits for TNP detection using CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs and other probes. This quantitative comparison includes key performance metrics such as limit of detection (LOD), linear range, response time, and recovery percentage, all of which are derived from experimental data in real-world samples. These metrics provide a comprehensive understanding of our method's performance compared to other techniques in the field. Additionally, to address the concern about detection mechanisms, we have included a detailed comparison of the detection mechanisms used in each method in Table S1. Our method, which utilizes a sustainable approach and fluorescence quenching technique, offers distinct advantages over existing methods, as represented in Table S1. We have also cited relevant literature to further substantiate the claims and demonstrate how our approach compares to current standards in TNP detection. This indicates that CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs exhibit superior sensitivity relative to previously documented TNP sensors.

The selectivity of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs toward TNP was confirmed by the Naked Eye-sensing experiments to further support the analysis. In this regard, TNP has concomitantly added to the H2O/pH 7.0 (v/v) solutions of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs. After the addition, the color of the MIPs nanocomposite changed significantly under UV light and daylight. In daylight, the colors of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs were changed from brown and turned light yellow in the presence of TNP (Fig. 8(a)). The observed color variations can be attributed to the increased electron deficiency of TNP. Moreover, Fig. 8(b) shows a significant vanishing in the bright blue emission of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs under UV light after the addition of TNP. These results once again clarify the effective sensing potential of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs for the TNP analyte.

Each template molecule possesses identical electronic and chemical structures; therefore, it is vital to investigate the selectivity of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs for the specific recognition of TNP. From this viewpoint, the fluorescence intensity of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs was tested against the analogs of TNP, such as hydroquinone, resorcinol, 3-NA (nitroaniline), 2,6- NP (dinitrophenol), catechol, and phenol; the results are presented in Fig. 8(c). In the presence of TNP analog, there is no/slight decrease in the fluorescence intensity, yet for 2,4-NP, the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs show quenching efficiency up to 61.77 %. This is due to the existence of the nitro group toward the imprinted sites present in the polymer matrix. Out of all the other analogs, the highest quenching efficiency is achieved for TNP. From this, it is evident that analogs of TNP did not affect the fluorescence intensity due to the lack of specific sites on the imprinted polymer (i.e., CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs. The fluorescence quenching efficiency percentage for each analyte vs CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs was represented in Fig. 8(d). These findings portray the high sensitivity and exceptional selectivity of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs for TNP. Beneficially, the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs quenching response for TNP is reproducible. Based on this point, the titration experiment is repeated four times, and the obtained results are presented in Fig. 8(e). The CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs displayed an effective fluorescence quenching response within 9 mins, which signifies their quick response time. TNP detection in real water is most important due to concentration and complexity. Spike and recovery experiments assessed CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs' durability and applicability, and further details are discussed in the supplementary material in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs as a smartphone-based optical bioassay for TNP
ce:section.ce:para: Further, the fluorescent images of the material under UV light were employed for TNP detection through a smartphone-based platform. As demonstrated in Fig. 9 , a smartphone functioned as the main tool for analysis. Following the addition of various concentrations of TNP, images of the solution were obtained under UV light using a smartphone camera. The images underwent processing through Color Detector software, which effectively extracted the RGB values from various samples. A linear calibration curve was plotted through the calculation of the blue-to-green RGB (G/B) ratio. The method demonstrated a strong linear regression within the TNP concentration range of 0.5 to 130 nM, resulting in an R2 value of 0.9617. Furthermore, the smartphone-based fluorescence sensor exhibited a limit of detection of 10.9 nM, which is lower than the maximum permissible levels established by both the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the World Health Organization (WHO). The repeated measurements with the same imaging setup yielded a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.8 %, indicating strong repeatability.

It is generally known that cobalt ferrite nanoparticles are a class of favorable peroxidase mimic behavior with the carbon nanodots. Furthermore, CF-MNPs show an outstanding capability due to their high surface area and stability toward environmental influences. Here the enhanced peroxidase-like behavior of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs was studied in a typical catalytic TMB oxidation reaction system. The CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs nanocomposite shows a color change in the presence of the chromogenic agent (TMB) and an oxidizing agent (H2O2) under a pH 4 phosphate buffer. Here, the CF-MNPs can act as catalysts in the presence of H2O2. The CNDs facilitate the oxidation of a TMB (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine) chromogenic substrate by H2O2, enhancing the electron transfer process during the oxidation reaction and making the catalytic activity more efficient. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded to confirm the peroxidase activity. The highest absorption occurs at 650 nm due to the oxidation of TMB by the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs with H2O2. For the control experiment, without the presence of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs or TMB, there is no color change visible, and the absorption intensity is nil. This shows that CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs show a peroxidase-like activity for the TMB oxidation under the H2O2 presence. This activity is compared with pristine CNDs, CF-MNPs, and CF-MNPs/CNDs/NIPs, and the results are presented in Fig. 10 . The CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs catalytic activity is 9.0 times higher than the other materials. The synergistic effect between the CNDs and CF-MNPs in the MIP causes enhanced peroxidase-like behavior.

The working mechanism, which is the reason for the peroxidase activity and the color change, is discussed here: Initially, the lone pair of electrons in the TMB is transferred to the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs, which makes it electron deficient. Now CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs transfer the excessive electron to the H2O2. CF-MNPs/CNDs can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and water (H2O). The generated •OH radicals then oxidize the TMB molecule, causing a color change from colorless to blue (oxidized TMB form). The EPR studies have been performed to confirm the generation of the •OH radicals and are presented in Fig. 10(c). These findings strongly suggested that •OH production drives the peroxidase mimic enzyme of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs. Then, an experiment was carried out to capture reactive oxygen species (ROS) and further study the way they engage in the catalytic reaction. This experiment examined TMB + H2O2 + CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs absorbance variations with different ROS scavengers. The experiment quantitatively assessed the variation in absorbance intensity with and without three specific scavengers: benzoquinone (BQ) (O2 •), D-Histidine (D-his) (1O2), and isopropanol (IPA) (•OH). The introduction of benzoquinone and D-Histidine did not result in any significant alteration in the absorbance value of the TMB + H2O2 + CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs system, indicating that O2 • and 1O2 were not substantially engaged in the catalytic reaction, as shown in Fig. 10(d). A notable reduction in absorbance intensity was observed upon the addition of IPA to the system. The observed decrease suggests that methanol effectively quenched the production of •OH, indicating the significant role of these radicals in the catalytic reaction. The results indicate that •OH is the primary reactive oxygen species driving the catalytic reaction. This discovery sheds light on the mechanics of the reaction and emphasizes the role of •OH in the system. The peroxidase-like activity of the synthesized CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs nanocomposite mimics the behavior of natural peroxidases like horseradish peroxidase (HRP), enabling sensitive detection of analytes through colorimetric assays under specific conditions like pH 4 phosphate buffer. Furthermore, to clarify the kinetics mechanism of the catalytic reaction, we examined the steady-state kinetics of the CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs nanozyme using the Michaelis-Menten equation. The results were discussed in the supporting material (Table: S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric binding constant calculation for TNP
ce:section.ce:para: The working mechanism for the detection of TNP using CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs nanozyme. The peroxidase–mimic CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs having nanozyme activity react with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to generate reactive species that can oxidize the peroxidase substrate TMB to a blue–colored product in acetate buffer (pH 4.0). The blue color of oxidized TMB arises from its capability to form a charge transfer complex with unoxidized TMB. This absorbance decreases at 650 nm, indicating colorimetric determination because TNP added to this medium interacts with CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs to partially inhibit their nanozyme activity, reverse the charge transfer–complex formation of TMB, and cause bleaching of the blue color by disrupting the charge transfer–complex formed from TMB. Considering the dependence of the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs, a precise and simple colorimetric approach was developed for the detection of TNP. The CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs+TMB + H2O2 reaction system absorbance intensity decreased over raising the concentration of TNP as shown in Fig. 10(e). The linear plot displays the change in the absorbance vs concentration of TNP. From this, the determination coefficient was found to be R2 = 0.9726, and the detection limit for TNP was found to be 3.07 nM as depicted in Fig.10(f and g). The optimization parameters for the analytical performance of the developed colorimetric sensor for TNP determination are summarized in Table S3. The limit of quantification (LOQ) was well below the EPA-recommended TNP residential screening level. These results imply the feasibility of the colorimetric approach for the detection of TNP.

In this work, we developed a novel and sustainable optical CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs bioassay for the detection of TNP. The CNDs were derived from the novel plant precursor Piper longum, contributing to the system's enhanced stability, sustainability, recyclability, and catalytic efficiency. The optical behavior of CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs was assessed using steady-state fluorescence and absorption spectra, revealing a distinct “turn-off” fluorescence response when exposed to TNP in fully aqueous media. CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs also possess exceptional catalytic performance, which efficiently decomposes H₂O₂ into hydroxyl radicals. However, TNP slows down the transfer of electrons to H₂O₂ by creating a surface complex on CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs, which lowers its ability to act like peroxidase. The suggested optical method showed very high sensitivity, with the lowest amount it could detect being 1.09 nM for the fluorometric assay and 3.07 nM for the colorimetric assay. Also, a fluorescence image taken with a smartphone app for color scanning allowed for the detection of TNP at a limit of 10 nM, providing an affordable, quick, and easy-to-use sensing method that is very selective and reliable. The successful detection of TNP in real-world samples further highlights the practical applicability of this system. These results emphasize that CF-MNPs/CNDs/MIPs could be a useful tool for accurately and sensitively detecting TNP in water samples and analyzing RGB colors using a smartphone app.

Komal Murugan: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Abirami Natarajan: Visualization, Supervision. Vetrinelaven Murthy Sridevi: Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All the authors declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was regarding the published Indian Patent (2022) “NANO COMPOSITE AND A PROCESS FOR ITS PREPARATION” (Application No. 202241051985 A). We acknowledge the Centre for Sustainable Material and Surface Metamorphosis, Chennai Institute of Technology, India, for partial funding (Funding number: CIT/CSMSM/2025/RP004) and acknowledge the Nanotechnology Research Centre (NRC), SRMIST, for providing the research facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jwpe.2025.107879.
